conclusion: Baker's framework is that AI demand keeps outrunning supply because the real constraints are physical: watts, wafers, and deployment logistics. He acknowledges bubble risk but says utilization evidence, Anthropic growth, and constrained foundry output point to continued scarcity. For investors, the key is tracking who controls bottlenecks — TSMC wafer discipline, near-100% GPU utilization, and SpaceX's orbital launch stack — and who can convert incremental compute into durable revenue fastest.
background: Gavin Baker returns to map the AI cycle through infrastructure rather than software multiples. He argues that recent market weakness masked accelerating usage signals, especially from Anthropic's reported growth. The conversation blends operating details and valuation logic: model economics, retention behavior, capex requirements, foundry constraints, and where returns accrue along the stack. Baker emphasizes that inference demand changed after reasoning-model progress, making compute scarcity more persistent than many expected. He also spends time on physical deployment constraints, from rack form factor and power draw to permitting and grid limitations. Those constraints motivate his interest in orbital compute as a potential long-term supplement for inference workloads, while training remains Earth-centric for now. On valuation, he repeatedly returns to one discipline point: estimate upside using constrained and unconstrained capacity scenarios, then size exposure based on who owns critical bottlenecks like advanced wafers and fully utilized accelerators. He also emphasizes that utilization data matters more than narrative in this phase: if frontier GPUs stay heavily loaded, customers are signaling real willingness to pay for inference and training throughput. In that environment, the risk is not a lack of ideas but a lack of physical delivery capacity. Baker therefore spends less time debating whether AI is useful and more time asking which constraints will relax first - foundry output, power interconnects, data-center buildouts, or
important_facts: Baker says Anthropic added roughly $11B of ARR in one month and links that to an unusually high reported dollar-retention figure near 500% from Krishna Rao's interview. He compares that monthly increment to what companies like Palantir, Snowflake, and Databricks took around 10-12 years to build, arguing that AI demand curves are operating on a different timescale.

He outlines a valuation envelope for Anthropic around $900B on $50B ARR, and says unconstrained revenue could potentially exceed $100B, $150B, or even $200B if compute supply were available. He also claims Anthropic burned roughly 80% less capital than OpenAI to reach similar revenue scale, suggesting stronger capital efficiency per unit of growth. Baker therefore spends less time debating whether AI is useful and more time asking which constraints will relax first - foundry output, power interconnects, data-center buildouts, or networking layers that allow compute to be placed in new geographies including orbit.

Blackwell racks weigh about 3,000 pounds — roughly 8 feet high by 4 feet deep by 3 feet wide — and draw about 100 kW. SpaceX controls roughly 98-99% of satellites in orbit; Starlink V3 runs around 20 kW. Under unconstrained TSMC capacity, Nvidia GPU sales could reach $2T-$3T in 2026-27.

mental_model.name: Watts-and-Wafers Constraint Map
mental_model.components: Demand evidence can diverge from stock action. Baker argues the March-April drawdown happened while core usage signals were strengthening, not weakening. His anchor example is Anthropic adding around $11B ARR in a month, which he treats as a fundamental data point too large to ignore. Orbital compute is an energy and permitting thesis. He does not pitch near-term replacement of terrestrial training clusters. Instead, he frames orbital inference as a potential response to Earth-based power bottlenecks, permitting delays, and rising deployment friction. TSMC capacity discipline shapes bubble outcomes. Baker says to watch TSMC's expansion choices. If capacity is too tight, customers face shortages; if capacity grows too aggressively, supply could outrun real demand and pressure returns.
mental_model.application: Baker applies this by favoring businesses that sit on bottlenecks rather than those merely consuming AI capacity. If GPUs are near 100% utilization and advanced-node wafers stay scarce, owners of supply chokepoints can capture outsized economics. He says unconstrained Nvidia GPU sales could reach $2T–$3T in 2026–27 if TSMC capacity allowed it, while Blackwell racks at ~100 kW and ~3,000 pounds illustrate Earth-side deployment friction that makes orbital inference a long-duration optional layer.

key_insights.view: Demand evidence can diverge from stock action.
key_insights.question: Why did he see the selloff as opportunity?
key_insights.answer: Baker argues the March-April drawdown happened while core usage signals were strengthening, not weakening. His anchor example is Anthropic adding around $11B ARR in a month, which he treats as a fundamental data point too large to ignore. The implication is that macro volatility can briefly discount infrastructure names even as underlying AI consumption remains supply-constrained and economically robust. He effectively treats the ARR print as a regime-change signal for valuation frameworks.

key_insights.view: Orbital compute is an energy and permitting thesis.
key_insights.question: Why does he take space data centers seriously?
key_insights.answer: He does not pitch near-term replacement of terrestrial training clusters. Instead, he frames orbital inference as a potential response to Earth-based power bottlenecks, permitting delays, and rising deployment friction. If launch economics continue to improve and satellite networking matures, off-Earth racks could absorb some incremental inference demand where latency and workload profile permit it. That optionality matters most if terrestrial grid additions remain slower than inference demand growth.

key_insights.view: TSMC capacity discipline shapes bubble outcomes.
key_insights.question: What signal best indicates overbuild risk?
key_insights.answer: Baker says to watch TSMC's expansion choices. If capacity is too tight, customers face shortages; if capacity grows too aggressively, supply could outrun real demand and pressure returns. His preferred outcome is controlled expansion that keeps utilization high enough to avoid a debt-fueled bust similar to earlier infrastructure manias, while still enabling stepwise growth in AI deployment.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Controls the wafer bottleneck and may prevent an AI infrastructure overbuild through disciplined capacity
Every GPU running at 100% utilization; latent demand could absorb far more supply if wafers existed
Starship, Starlink lasers, and orbital compute could turn space into a real inference infrastructure layer

golden_quotes: Anthropic added in one month roughly what Palantir, Snowflake, and Databricks spent a decade building.
For AI, we are going to consume as much compute as we can; the limit is watts and wafers.
If I had to watch one thing to understand whether there is an AI bubble, it would be Taiwan Semi's capacity decisions.
